title: American Girl Dollhouse (Fits 18" Dolls)
summary: A six-foot, three-story dollhouse scaled for 18" dolls — two sheets of plywood, real rooms a kid can reach into, and caster wheels because it's furniture-sized.
description:
Eighteen-inch dolls have a scale problem: 'dollhouses' at the store fit dolls a third their size. This build solves it with architecture — a 73" tall, 48" wide, 23-3/4" deep three-story house where an American Girl doll stands in her own kitchen. It's two sheets of 3/4" plywood (or MDF, which paints beautifully but weighs more), a 1/4" back, and a 1x4 roof.

The 45° roof layout is solved with plain geometry, and the plan hands it to you: find the top center of each tall panel (11-7/8" from the sides), measure down 11-7/8", connect the points — that diagonal IS the 45° roofline. Windows and doors get marked and jigsawed at the same flat-on-sawhorses stage, which is the only time cutting them is easy.

Two practical notes from the source build: have the store rip the sheets to 23-3/4" if you can't haul plywood (the crosscuts at home are then manageable), and put the thing on CASTERS — a six-foot dollhouse gets moved constantly, and wheels are the difference between furniture and installation art. Window trim is fitted piece by piece and glued; shutters are a 1x2 flourish for whenever the mood strikes.

tools.note: Breaking down two full sheets — or have the store rip them to 23-3/4" widths.
Every window and door opening. Cut them while the panels are flat.
3/4" setting, 1-1/4" screws — all the carcass joinery.
Every opening's edge — small hands reach through these windows.

materials.name: Plywood or MDF, 3/4", 4x8 sheet
materials.note: MDF paints smoother; plywood is lighter and holds screws better.

materials.name: 1x4, 12 ft
materials.note: Roof and base trim — one more if trimming the base in wood instead of adding casters.

materials.name: Caster wheels
materials.note: The source's choice over a trimmed base — a 6-foot dollhouse moves a lot.

cutList.note: Tops cut to the 45° roof angle — layout method in step 1.
Or one 3/4" plywood panel at 49-1/2" x 17-1/2", measured and cut to fit.

steps.title: Cut everything flat — including windows
steps.body:
Break down the sheets (or have the store rip them to 23-3/4" widths and just crosscut at home). Longest pieces first.

The 45° roof peak, no protractor: find the top center of each tall panel — 11-7/8" from either side — measure DOWN 11-7/8" on the sides, and connect the marks. That diagonal is your 45°.

Mark every window and door now and cut them with the jigsaw while the panels lie flat. A window cut into an assembled dollhouse is a punishment; the same window cut on sawhorses is a pleasure.

steps.title: Join the walls to the bottom
steps.body: Attach the two sides and center divider to the bottom panel — 3/4"-setting pocket holes with 1-1/4" screws, holes facing down and inward where dolls won't testify to them.

steps.title: Install the floors
steps.body: Add the four upper floor panels with pocket holes drilled on their UNDERSIDES — invisible from every doll's-eye view. Floor heights set the room ceilings; an 18" doll wants about 22" of clearance to stand with hair intact.

steps.title: Roof one slope
steps.body: The source made the top floor one big room, which leaves no center bearing under the ridge — so a plywood roof panel works for the big slope: glue and nail it with the top edge flush at the peak. (Going all-1x4 works too if you kept the upper divider.)

steps.title: Build and set the front roof panel
steps.body: Pocket-hole the 1x4s into a single roof panel flat on the bench, then set and fasten the whole panel to the front slope. Building it as a unit first is why its board gaps stay even.

steps.title: Close the back
steps.body: Glue and nail the 1/4" back into every wall and floor edge it crosses. Beyond keeping dolls from falling out the back, the panel is the shear web that stops a six-foot case from racking when it's rolled.

steps.title: Casters (or base trim)
steps.body: Screw the four casters to the bottom corners — the source's pick, and the right one for a piece that weighs as much as a dresser and lives in a kid's room. Prefer the furniture look? Trim the base in 1x4 instead.

steps.title: Trim the windows
steps.body: Measure each window opening and build its 1x2 trim to fit snugly, gluing the pieces in place. Fitted one-by-one because jigsawed openings vary — the trim is what makes each one look intentional.

steps.title: Paint, and shutters someday
steps.body:
Prime and paint — this is the step where it becomes THE dollhouse, so let the resident designer pick colors. 1x2 shutters flanking the windows are the plan's someday-flourish; glue them on whenever the renovation budget allows.

Sand every opening edge a hand will reach through before handover. Then stand back: you built a house.

images.alt: Large three-story wooden dollhouse scaled for 18-inch dolls
